title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
To understand the 2010s music landscape, we utilized the MusicOSet dataset, which provides an enhanced collection of musical elements perfect for data mining. Our analysis relied on three primary files:

hits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 11,959 songs that appeared on the Billboard Hot 100.
nonhits_dataset.csv: Features for 899,068 songs by “hit artists” (artists that had at least one charting song) that did not chart.
artists.csv: Metadata for 11,518 artists, including their associated genres.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
To understand the 2010s music landscape, we utilized the MusicOSet dataset, which provides an enhanced collection of musical elements perfect for data mining. Our analysis relied on three primary files:

hits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 11,959 songs that appeared on the Billboard Hot 100.
nonhits_dataset.csv: Features for 899,068 songs by “hit artists” (artists that had at least one charting song) that did not chart.
artists.csv: Metadata for 11,518 artists, including their associated genres.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
The Cleaning Process

1. Broadening the Genre Spectrum
The raw main_genre data was often too specific or niche for effective modeling. We used keyword searches to group these into broader categories such as ‘Pop’, ‘Hip Hop/Rap’, ‘Rock/Metal’, and ‘Electronic/Dance’. Any genres with fewer than 100 observations were moved into an ‘Other’ category to maintain statistical significance.


2. Standardizing the 2010s Scope
Because our project focuses on the 2010s, we applied several strict filters:

Timeframe: We only kept tracks with a release date between 2010 and 2019.
Consistency: We dropped songs by artists who did not have at least one hit during this specific decade (to create two comparable datasets where popularity of the arist during this period is less of a factor).
Outlier Removal: We identified and removed certain “artists” who uploaded extremely long mixes of other songs, which skewed our duration_ms column.



3. Balancing the Dataset
The raw data was heavily skewed toward non-hits (nearly 900,000 vs. 12,000 hits). To create a fair classification model, we:

Took a random sample of non-hits equal to the number of hits.
This resulted in a final combined_music_data set of 8,132 songs (4,066 hits and 4,066 non-hits).



4. Normalization
To ensure our model wasn’t influenced by the different scale for certain measurements, we normalized multiple key variables—loudness, tempo, and duration_ms—onto a scale of 0 to 1.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
Final Dataset Structure
After cleaning, our dataset consisted of 8,132 observations across 16 variables. Below is the summary of our primary explanatory variables after normalization:



Variable
Mean
Std. Deviation




duration_ms
0.170
0.050


danceability
0.655
0.147


energy
0.686
0.173


loudness
0.774
0.076


acousticness
0.160
0.207



Note: The tight distribution of duration shows that most successful songs in this era adhered to a very specific length

title: MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science Project
text:
Hello! We are the Danish Club, made up of Vassar College senior mathematics majors Anders Hansen and Miles Sondergaard Jensen. Welcome to our MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science final project report. Navigate the tabs at the top to explore our analysis of what separates hit songs from non-hit songs!





Image from rawpixel

title: Introduction
text:
In the music industry, there is a long-standing debate: is a song’s success driven by its tangible musical characteristics, or is it a product of “unmeasurables” like marketing, catchiness, pure luck, or timing, among others?
Our project seeks to address this by analyzing a comprehensive sample of hit and non-hit songs released between 2010 and 2019. We define a “hit” specifically as any song that made an appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 at any point after its release


We chose to focus on the 2010s for several reasons:

Formative Years: This decade represents the childhood and teenage years for many in our generation, making the music personally resonant.
Lasting Impact: The music from this era was exceptionally high-quality, with many tracks still charting or receiving many streams today.
Shifting Trends: The criteria for what makes a song “successful” appeared to undergo significant shifts during this ten-year window.




The primary objective of this study is to predict the success of 2010s songs based on their specific audio characteristics. We aimed to build a classification model capable of differentiating hit songs from non-hit songs with at least 50% accuracy (better than just random guessing!).



Going into our modeling phase, we developed two sets of expectations regarding Spotify’s audio features:

Predictors of Success: We hypothesized that higher danceability, valence (musical positiveness), and loudness would significantly increase a song’s likelihood of becoming a hit. Conversely, we expected lower duration, acousticness, speechiness, and instrumentalness to be associated with chart success.
Neutral Elements: We predicted that features like key, tempo, mode, and whether a song is explicit would have little to no measurable effect on a song’s probability of charting.


Next, we will dive into the Data and Cleaning process to show how we narrowed down nearly a million observations into a clean, balanced dataset for our analysis and modeling.
